name: Kenny Dorham
description: American jazz trumpeter, singer, and composer (1924–1972)
abstract: McKinley Howard "Kenny" Dorham (August 30, 1924 – December 5, 1972) was an American jazz trumpeter, composer, and occasional singer. Dorham's talent is frequently lauded by critics and other musicians, but he never received the kind of attention or public recognition from the jazz establishment that many of his peers did.

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Heath
about_quotes.evidence: Many years ago I had a band with Kenny Dorham, Cedar Walton, Sonny Redd on alto, and Pepper Adams on the baritone.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Heath — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Art Farmer
about_quotes.evidence: Then, after those three came Kenny Dorham. Those are the ones that really influenced me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Art Farmer — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Golson
about_quotes.evidence: Here’s one called The Modern Touch. Benny Golson sextet, right? Jay Jay Johnson, Kenny Dorham, Wynton Kelly, Paul Chambers, and on drums, Max Roach. ... It was Kenny Dorham, J. J. Johnson, Wynton Kelly, and Paul Chambers. He was the drummer. ... that was the only time we actually played together.
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Golson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Sam Jones
about_quotes.evidence: Kenny Dorham heard me up there playing... Say 'I'm fixing to get a band out.'... so we uh, then we went to--first gig was out in Cleveland somewhere.

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Woods
about_quotes.evidence: I was invited to be part of the Birdland All-Stars... and Kenny Dorham and Conte Candoli, two trumpets and tenor and alto.
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Woods interviewed by Monk Rowe, Delaware Water Gap, PA, November 8, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Abbey Lincoln
about_quotes.evidence: I recorded the first album I made with Max and Kenny Dorham and Sonny Rollins and Wynton Kelly and Paul Chambers
about_quotes.doc_title: Abbey Lincoln — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
